title: Business ownership and sources of finance
summary: 9609 AS — how sole trader, partnership, Ltd and PLC ownership affects access to finance.
learningObjectives: Link ownership types to available finance options.
Compare sole trader, partnership, and company funding.
Explain basic equity vs debt at AS level.
Apply ownership constraints to case scenarios.

simpleExplanation.title: Structure shapes funding
simpleExplanation.summary: How a business is owned determines how much capital it can raise, from whom, and whether owners face unlimited liability.
simpleExplanation.analogy: A sole trader's business wallet is tied to their personal wallet — banks may lend less because one person carries all risk. A public company can ask thousands of shareholders to invest, but owners share control.
steps: Unincorporated: unlimited liability, limited formal finance.
PLC: can issue shares to the public.
Ownership trade-off: control vs access to capital.

flashcards.back: Personal savings, bank loan/overdraft, friends/family — unlimited liability limits scale.
Share capital (private issue), retained profit, bank loans, leasing, trade credit.
Can raise large sums via public share issue (IPO/FPO) on stock exchange.
Owner personally liable for all business debts — personal assets at risk.
Shareholders lose only their investment in the company — not personal wealth beyond that.
Money from owners/shareholders — no mandatory repayment but dilutes control.
Borrowed funds — must be repaid with interest; no dilution if secured.
Business ownership topic explains legal forms; 5.2.1 links them to funding.

sections.content: **Business ownership and finance** (5.2.1) connects **1.2.2 business ownership** to **5.2.2 sources**. Case questions often describe a **sole trader wanting to expand** — you must explain why **incorporation** or **PLC status** opens new finance routes.

items: **Sole trader / partnership:** Easy to set up; **unlimited liability**; finance mainly personal or small bank facilities.
**Private Ltd:** Separate legal entity; **limited liability**; can sell shares privately; better access to loans.
**PLC:** Can sell shares to public; greater capital but more regulation and potential **loss of control**.

sections.content:
**Equity** — share capital, retained profit. No fixed repayment; new shareholders may reduce original owners' control.

**Debt** — loans, overdrafts, debentures. Fixed interest and repayment; risk if cash flow weakens (**gearing** — 10.2.4).

sections.question: A successful sole trader wants $500 000 to open three new shops. Explain two finance constraints linked to ownership.
sections.solution:
**1. Unlimited liability** — banks may limit lending because the owner's personal assets secure the debt; a large loan is riskier for both parties.

**2. No share issue** — unlike a **Ltd or PLC**, a sole trader **cannot sell shares** to raise $500k quickly. Options are mainly **retained profit**, **bank loan**, or **converting to a company** to access equity finance.

Recommendation might include **incorporation** to limit liability and enable share investment.
